docs.title: Documentation for pynxtools-igor
docs.text:
pynxtools-igor is a free, and open-source data software for converting data stored with Wavemetrics Igor Pro into the NeXus format. It is implemented as a plugin for pynxtools and allows to read, translate, and standardize data from Igor Pro binary waves and packed experiment files. Depending on the domain of data, pynxtools allows to ensure compliance with various NeXus application definitions, e.g. NXmpes for angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) experiments, or NXxrd for x-ray diffraction experiments.

pynxtools-igor is developed both as a standalone reader and as a tool within NOMAD, which is the open-source data management platform for materials science we are developing within FAIRmat.

Contact

For questions or suggestions:

  • Open an issue on the pynxtools-igor GitHub
  • Join our Discord channel
  • Get in contact with our lead developers.
Project and community
  • NOMAD code guidelines

The work is funded by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG, German Research Foundation) - 460197019 (FAIRmat).

docs.title: Get in contact
docs.text:
NOMAD, pynxtools, and pynxtools-igor are open source projects that warmly welcome community projects, contributions, suggestions, bug fixes, and constructive feedback. pynxtools is built mainly within FAIRmat Area B - Experiment.

You can reach us through different channels. You can send an email directly to one of the main contributors:

Name E-mail Github profiles Dr. Lukas Pielsticker lukas.pielsticker@physik.hu-berlin.de @lukaspie Prof. Dr. Laurenz Rettig rettig@fhi-berlin.mpg.de / l.rettig@rptu.de @rettigl

Alternatively, you can also:

  • Open an issue on GitHub
  • Join the NOMAD discord channel and ask us there directly.

docs.title: Note on versioning
docs.text: Keeping track of versions is essential for every software and semantic data modeling activity. We follow PEP440 when reporting versions, fetching metadata at run time using importlib.metadata. Several versions need to be distinguished. The version of pynxtools, the one of the NeXus definitions, and those of h5py and the HDF5 library. See the respective section of the pynxtools documentation that explains where these versions can be found.

docs.title: pynxtools-plugin version
docs.text: We consider it best practice that also each plugin of pynxtools writes its version as a programID instance of NXprogram. ID serves as a placeholder that can be either omitted or replaced by an integer if multiple programs need to be distinguished. Counting should start at one, i.e., program or program1 are preferred options. Specifically, the field program inside the programID instance should store the distribution (package) name. The attribute program/@version should store a version string following PEP440.

docs.title: Data from Igor Pro
docs.text: The igor reader allows reading data from Wavemetrics Igor Pro .ibw "binary wave" files and .pxp "packed experiment" files. These two modes of operation are mutually exclusive, i.e. you can either pass one or multiple ibw files, or one pxp file. The behavior of the reader is controlled via two config files: config.json defines the assignment to nexus class paths, whereas entries.yaml.entry defines which data to read from the source file(s). Here we explain several examples how to use the reader. These examples convert to the nexus class NXroot, the most generic Nexus class, but any other NeXus application definition can also be used.

docs.title: Convert from .ibw Igor binary wave files
docs.text:
The conversion from .ibw files does not require the definition of an entry file. In that case, each ibw file generates an entry with the filename (excluding ".ibw") as entry name. Axis data as well as units are read from the ibw wave information (internal wave scaling), and named axis0 to axis3. These are available via the @data mechanism. Wave notes are parsed and split according to a key=value\\n schema, and are available vie the @attrs mechanism.

Alternatively, an entry definition can be passed in addition, which contains a dictionary of entries, where each entry key generates an entry of that name. The dict entries are expected to be dicts as well, containing at least the key data with the filename of the respective ibw file to use as data for this entry. Additionally, axisN_name keys can be passed to rename the axis entries from the default axis0 to axis3. Similarly, axisN_units and data_units can be passed to overwrite respective information read from the ibw files. ibw files for which no entry has been defined will generate an entry according to their filename as before. Additionally, each entry can contain a metadata dict with additional metadata specific to this entry.

This entry dict can be passed as an object, in which case it is expected as sole and single object in the objects tuple. Alternatively, the entry dict can also be passed as yaml file with extension .entry.

docs.title: Conversion from Igor packed experiment files
docs.text:
Conversion from .pxp files requires definition of an entry dict. Here also, each key/value defines one entry to generate in the Nexus file. the data entry contains here the ibw-path to the wave to use as main data entry. Axis information can be either read from the wave scaling, or be provided via an axisN entry, pointing to a wave containing axis information. Similarly, a data_errors key can be defined, pointing to a wave containing data uncertainties.

The reader for Igor Pro data can be found here.

Example data for the igor reader is available here.

The example conversion can be run with the following command. 

user@box:~$ 
dataconverter --reader igor --nxdl NXroot --output example_pxp.nxs config_file.json Fig2a.pxp Scan57_59.yaml.entry

The reader is a tailored parser for research data in a common format. This particular example is able to read and map HDF5 files, as well as JSON and YAML files. Feel free to contact FAIRmat if you want to create a parser for your research data.

docs.title: Development guide
docs.text: This tutorial will guide you through on how to set up a working environment for developing pynxtools-igor.

docs.title: What should you know before this tutorial?
docs.text:
• You should read the guide on getting started with pynxtools.
  • You should read the installation tutorial.

docs.title: What you will know at the end of this tutorial?
docs.text:
You will know

  • how to setup your environment for developing pynxtools-igor
  • how to make changes to the software
  • how to test the software
  • how to contribute on GitHub
  • how to use pynxtools-igor as a NOMAD plugin

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
Structure of the pynxtools-igor repository

The software tools are located inside src/pynxtools_igor. They are shipped with unit tests located in tests.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
It is recommended to use python 3.12 with a dedicated virtual environment for this package. Learn how to manage python versions and virtual environments. We recommend using uv, an extremely fast Python package and project manager. In this tutorial, you will find parallel descriptions, using either uv or a more classical approach using venv and pip.

Start by creating a virtual environment:

uvvenv

uv is capable of creating a virtual environment and install the required Python version at the same time.

uv venv --python 3.12

Note that you will need to install the Python version manually beforehand.

python -m venv .venv

That command creates a new virtual environment in a directory called .venv.

docs.title: Development installation
docs.text:
We start by cloning the repository:

git clone https://github.com/FAIRmat-NFDI/pynxtools-igor.git \\
    --branch main \\
    --recursive pynxtools-igor
cd pynxtools-igor

Next, we install the package in editable mode (together with its dependencies):

uvpip 
uv pip install -e ".[dev]"

Note that you will need to install the Python version manually beforehand.

pip install --upgrade pip
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Linting and formatting
docs.text:
We are using ruff and mypy for linting, formatting, and type checking. It is recommended to use the pre-commit hook available for ruff which formats the code and checks the linting before making a Git commit.

Install the precommit by running

pre-commit install

from the root of this repository.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
There exist unit tests for the software written in pytest which can be used as follows:

pytest -sv tests

docs.title: Editing the documentation
docs.text:
We are using `mkdocs for the documentation. If you edit the documentation, you can build it locally. For this, you need to install an additional set of dependencies:

uvpip 
uv pip install -e ".[docs]"

pip install -e ".[docs]"

You can then serve the documentation locally by running

mkdocs serve

docs.title: Contributing to the package on GitHub
docs.text: Once you are happy with the changes, please commit them on a separate branch and create a pull request on GitHub. We run a number of GitHub actions that check the correct linting, run the tests in an isolated environment, and build the documentation. Once these pass and a peer review of the code has occurred, your code will be accepted.

docs.title: Developing pynxtools-igor as a NOMAD plugin
docs.text: If you plan to contribute to the NOMAD plugin functionality of pynxtools-igor, it often makes sense to use the NOMAD development environment called nomad-distro-dev. You can learn more in the NOMAD documentation.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: If you face any issues with the tool or when setting up the development environment, please create a new Github Issue.

docs.title: What should you know before this tutorial?
docs.text:
To get started, it does not hurt to read the following pynxtools tutorial:

  • Guide on getting started with pynxtools, NeXus, and NOMAD
  • Installation tutorial for pynxtools

docs.title: What you will know at the end of this tutorial?
docs.text:
You will know

  • how to install pynxtools-igor
  • how to install pynxtools-igor together with NOMAD

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
It is recommended to use python 3.12 with a dedicated virtual environment for this package. Learn how to manage python versions and virtual environments.

There are many alternatives to managing virtual environments and package dependencies (requirements). We recommend using uv, an extremely fast Python package and project manager. In this tutorial, you will find parallel descriptions, using either uv or a more classical approach using venv and pip.

Start by creating a virtual environment:

uvvenv

uv is capable of creating a virtual environment and install the required Python version at the same time.

uv venv --python 3.12

Note that you will need to install the Python version manually beforehand.

python -m venv .venv

That command creates a new virtual environment in a directory called .venv.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install the latest stable version of this package from PyPI with

uvpip 
uv pip install pynxtools-igor

pip install pynxtools-igor

You can also install the latest development version with

uvpip 
uv pip install git+https://github.com/FAIRmat-NFDI/pynxtools-igor.git

pip install git+https://github.com/FAIRmat-NFDI/pynxtools-igor.git

docs.title: How to install pynxtools-igor with NOMAD
docs.text: To use pynxtools-igor with NOMAD, simply install it in the same environment as the nomad-lab package. NOMAD will recognize pynxtools-igor as a plugin automatically. In addition, NOMAD will install a schema for NeXus application definitions.

docs.title: Start using pynxtools-igor
docs.text: That's it! You can now use pynxtools-igor!

docs.title: Convert Igor pro data and metadata to NeXus
Who is this tutorial for?
docs.text: This document is for people who want to use this reader as a standalone application for converting their research data into a standardized NeXus format.

docs.title: What should you should know before this tutorial?
docs.text:
• You should have a basic understanding of FAIRmat NeXus and pynxtools
  • You should have a basic understanding of using Python and Jupyter notebooks via JupyterLab

docs.title: What you will know at the end of this tutorial?
docs.text: You will have a basic understanding how to use pynxtools-igor for converting your Igor Pro data to a NeXus/HDF5 file.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: See the installation guide for how to install pynxtools together with the Igor reader plugin.

docs.title: Running the reader from the command line
docs.text:
An example script to run the igor reader in pynxtools: 

 ! dataconverter \
--reader igor \
--nxdl NXroot \
$<igor-file path> \
$<eln-file path> \
$<entry-file path> \
-c $<config-file path> \
--output <output-file path>.nxs

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
You can find various examples how to use pynxtools-igor for your Igor Pro research data pipeline in src/pynxtools-igor/nomad/examples. These are designed for working with NOMAD and its NOMAD Remote Tools Hub (NORTH).

There are also small example files for using the pynxtools dataconverter with the igor reader in tests/data.

For this tutorial, we will work with this data. You can run the conversion as

dataconverter \\
    --reader igor \\
    --nxdl NXroot \\
    Norm_0057.ibw \\
    Norm_0059.ibw \\
    -c  config_file.json \\
    --output example_ibw.nxs

Congrats! You now have a NeXus file generated from the Igor binary wave files!
